On 5 February 2025, the former residence of [[Sheikh Mujibur Rahman]], commonly known as [[Bangabandhu Memorial Museum|Dhanmondi 32]], was occupied and demolished by the protesters in [[Dhaka, Bangladesh]]. The building, which housed [[Bangabandhu Memorial Museum|a museum]] and was considered a historic site by the [[Awami League]] supporters, was torn down by protesters during a period of intense political turmoil following months after the [[resignation of Sheikh Hasina|resignation]] of the [[Prime Minister of Bangladesh|Prime Minister]] [[Sheikh Hasina]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.dhakatribune.com/bangladesh/dhaka/372963/chronology-of-dhanmondi-32-destruction|title=Timeline: The demolition of Sheikh Mujib's Dhanmondi 32 residence|author=Tribune desk|date=6 February 2025|website=[[Dhaka Tribune]]|access-date=6 February 2025}}</ref>
The demolition followed an online press conference attended by Sheikh Hasina while in [[Student-People's uprising|self-imposed exile]] in [[India]], during which she addressed topics that protesters viewed as against national interests. This event occurred amid years of growing discontent regarding the political influence of the [[Tungipara Sheikh family|Sheikh family]] in the country's political landscape, and the rise of a [[Sheikh Mujibur Rahman's cult of personality|cult of personality of Sheikh Mujibur Rahman]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news/bangladesh/news/pictures-demolition-dhanmondi-32-residence-3817416|title=In pictures: Demolition of Dhanmondi-32 residence|author=Star Digital Report|date=6 February 2025|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=6 February 2025}}</ref>
Despite these events, the structure remained standing until the subsequent demolition in February 2025. In February 2025, online activist and YouTuber [[Pinaki Bhattacharya]] encouraged his followers to demolish the house.<ref>{{Cite web |last=ডেস্ক |first=কালবেলা |title=এবার ১ মিনিটের ভিডিও বার্তা পিনাকীর {{!}} কালবেলা |url=https://www.kalbela.com/social-media/161766 |access-date=2025-02-06 |website=কালবেলা {{!}} বাংলা নিউজ পেপার |language=bn-BD}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=টেলিভিশন |first=Ekushey TV {{!}} একুশে |title=আওয়াজ তোলেন থাকবে না, ৩২ নাম্বার থাকবে না: পিনাকী |url=https://www.ekushey-tv.com/social-media/1360873111111157690 |access-date=2025-02-06 |website=Ekushey TV |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=জনকণ্ঠ |first=দৈনিক |title=ধানমন্ডি ৩২ ভাঙ্গার জন্য কি পোস্ট করেছেন পিনাকী? |

'''Tariq Ramadan''' ({{langx|ar|طارق رمضان}}, {{IPA|ar|tˤaːriq ramadˤaːn|}}; born 26 August 1962) is a Swiss Muslim academic, philosopher and writer.<ref name="guardian" /> He has held academic positions including professor of contemporary Islamic studies at [[St Antony's College, Oxford]] and the [[Faculty of Theology and Religion, University of Oxford]].<ref name="ProfessorialFellow"/><ref name="FTR"/> He has also been affiliated with institutions including [[Doshisha University]], [[Université Mundiapolis]], and the Research Centre of Islamic Legislation and Ethics in Doha.<ref name="CileCenter"/>
Ramadan is the grandson of [[Hassan al-Banna]], the founder of the [[Muslim Brotherhood]], and is known  for his writings and lectures on Islam, Muslim identity in Europe, and reformist thought.<ref name="oxford" /> He has described himself as a "[[Salafi movement|Salafi]] reformist".<ref>{{cite magazine | url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/how-the-tariq-ramadan-scandal-derailed-the-balancetonporc-movement-in-france | title=How the Tariq Ramadan Scandal Derailed the #Balancetonporc Movement in France | access-date=10 December 2017 | last=Shatz | first=Adam | magazine=The New Yorker | archive-date=9 December 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171209081357/https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/how-the-tariq-ramadan-scandal-derailed-the-balancetonporc-movement-in-france | url-status=live}}</ref>

Ramadan has been convicted of [[rape]] and [[Sexual assault|sexual coercion]] in Switzerland and of rape and rape of a [[Vulnerable adult|vulnerable person]] in France.<ref name="c282"/><ref name="a356"/> In 2024, a Swiss appeals court convicted him in relation to a 2008 incident in Geneva, the conviction which became final in 2025 after his appeal was rejected by the [[Federal Supreme Court of Switzerland]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=10 September 2024 |title=Tariq Ramadan convicted of rape in Switzerland |work=BBC|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cx2n74n6q1vo |access-date=10 September 2024}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |date=2025-08-28 |title=Switzerland's top court upholds Islamic scholar Tariq Ramadan's conviction for rape, sexual coercion |url=https://apnews.com/article/tariq-ramadan-switzerland-rape-dc3897baa9e54a4d629e9f89e6d55677 |access-date=2025-08-28 |website=AP News}}</ref> In March 2026, the [[Tribunal de Paris|Paris criminal court]] convicted him in relation to offences committed between 2009 and 2016 and sentenced him to 18 years' imprisonment; the judgment is not final and may be appealed.<ref name="c282"/><ref name="a356"/>

{{Nihongo|'''''Corpse Party'''''|コープスパーティー|'''''Kōpusu Pātī'''''|lead=yes}} is a [[Horror game|horror video game]] originally created by Makoto Kedōin<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.memoriesoffear.com/games_corpsepartypc98/index.html|title=Memories of Fear...''Corpe Party'' Rebuilt|website=memoriesoffear.com|access-date=2014-10-17|archive-date=2014-07-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140706100042/http://www.memoriesoffear.com/games_corpsepartypc98/index.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> and eventually developed by Team GrisGris.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.gris2.com/gris_product.htm|title=Products|publisher=Team GrisGris|access-date=24 October 2015|language=ja|archive-date=4 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304063317/http://www.gris2.com/gris_product.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> Blending elements and genres of [[Supernatural|supernatural horror]], [[survival horror]], [[Adventure game|adventure]], and [[doujin soft]], the video game often features high school students who attempt to escape after becoming trapped in an abandoned, parallel school, haunted by a vengeful spirit in a red dress.
The video game has spawned several [[Manga|mangas]], an [[anime]] [[OVA]], [[Drama CD|drama CDs]], [[Sunshine City, Tokyo#Namco Namja Town|theme park]] attractions, and two [[Corpse Party (film)|live action films]].
==History==
The first game in the franchise was developed using the [[RPG Maker]] [[game development tool|software]] version RPG Tsukūru Dante 98 and released on April 22, 1996, for the [[PC-9801]]. It was followed by three remakes:
#''Corpse Party: Blood Covered... Repeated Fear'',{{efn|name=''RepeatedFear''}} which was released for the [[PlayStation Portable]] on August 12, 2010, and [[iOS]] on February 9, 2012. It was released on PC Steam in 2021, under the title ''Corpse Party (2021)''.
The game was released in North America and Europe by [[Xseed Games]] and [[Marvelous (company)|Marvelous Europe]] under the title ''Corpse Party''.<ref name="Anime News Network">{{cite web|url=https://www.animenewsnetwork.com/interest/2011-09-02/xseed-licenses-corpse-party-horror-psp-game|first=Jennifer|last=Sherman|title=Marvelous USA Licenses ''Corpse Party'' Horror PSP Game|date=September 3, 2011|access-date=September 3, 2011|website=[[Anime News Network]]|archive-date=August 26, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210826115631/https://www.animenewsnetwork.com/interest/2011-09-02/xseed-licenses-corpse-party-horror-psp-game|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Xseed Games]] planned to release a localized version of ''Corpse Party: Blood Covered'' for PC in North America in 2015,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.siliconera.com/2014/06/05/xseed-bringing-corpse-party-pc-2014/|title=Xseed Bringing ''Corpse Party'' To PC In 2014|website=Siliconera|date=June 5, 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140608003236/https://www.siliconera.com/2014/06/05/xseed-bringing-corpse-party-pc-2014/|archive-

== Associated Folklores ==
=== Folk tale of Lalazar ===
The folk tale of Lala Afghani and Sohni (Lalazar) is intricately linked to the romantic saga of an Afghan youth named Lala and a local maiden named ''Sohni''. Rooted in the cultural fabric of the region, this narrative unfolds as Lala, while passing through the village, becomes enamored with Sohni. However, societal disapproval prevails, leading to the tragic demise of the young man at the hands of the local community.

The site of Lala's sacrifice has since gained renown as Lalazar, with "Zar" signifying the location where Lala made the ultimate sacrifice for his love. Notably, the adjacent forest is christened Sohni da Banr, translating to the forest of ''Sohni''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Arif |first1=Anas Mahmud |last2=Hameed |first2=Abdul |last3=Fizza |first3=Syeda |date=2021-09-30 |title=Cultural Tourism in Kaghan Valley: Potential and Problems |journal=Pakistan Journal of Social Research |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=23–43 |doi=10.52567/pjsr.v3i3.223 |issn=2710-3129|doi-access=free }}</ref>

In contemporary times, Lalazar has evolved into a well-known village, emerging as a burgeoning tourist destination within the valley. Simultaneously, Sohni da Banr retains its prominence as a distinguished forest in the area, preserving the essence of the timeless tale of Lala Afghani and Sohni. The rich cultural heritage associated with Lalazar continues to attract visitors, offering a glimpse into the enduring allure of this captivating narrative.<ref name=":0" />

=== Folk tale of Munshi and Darshi (Paras) ===
The romantic saga of Munshi and Darshi (Paras) unfolds against the backdrop of a renowned village called Paras in the Kaghan Valley.<ref name=":0" /> During the British colonial era, Munshi, employed as a worker for a company, toiled in the local forest. It was here that he became entangled in a love affair with Darshi, a girl from the community. The clandestine meetings of Munshi and Darshi became the talk of the town, with their love story gaining prominence in the region.

However, societal resistance prevailed, and Munshi faced rejection from the locals, ultimately meeting a tragic end at their hands. The site of this fateful event garnered fame as Darshi da Banr, signifying the forest of Darshi. This woodland has since become a popular destination within the valley, celebrated for its breathtaking beauty, diverse wildlife, and suitability for hiking adventures.

The '''Cabinet of Morocco''' is the chief executive body of the [[Morocco|Kingdom of Morocco]]. It is composed of ministers, including ministers delegate, and is headed by the [[Prime Minister of Morocco|Prime Minister]].
Since the adoption of the 2011 Constitution, the Prime Minister, officially titled Head of Government, is appointed by the [[King of Morocco]] from the political party that won the most seats in the [[Elections in Morocco|parliamentary elections]]. The members of the Cabinet are proposed by the Head of Government, in consultation with coalition parties, and are subsequently appointed by the King.
As of October 2021, the Cabinet is headed by [[Aziz Akhannouch]], appointed by [[Mohammed VI of Morocco|King Mohammed VI]] following the [[2021 Moroccan general election]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.moroccoworldnews.com/2021/09/344390/aziz-akhannouch-moroccos-new-billionaire-prime-minister|title=Aziz Akhannouch: Morocco's New Billionaire Prime Minister|first=Shaquile|last=Goff|publisher=Morocco World News|date=19 September 2021|accessdate=18 September 2021}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|title=Le Roi Mohammed VI nomme Aziz Akhannouch chef du gouvernement|language=fr-FR|website=Medias24|url=https://medias24.com/2021/09/10/le-roi-mohammed-vi-nomme-aziz-akhannouch-chef-du-gouvernement/|date=10 September 2021|accessdate=18 September 2021}}</ref> The current government includes 24 ministers, among them several ministers delegate, and was sworn in on 7 October 2021.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.aa.com.tr/en/middle-east/moroccan-king-names-new-government-headed-by-aziz-akhannouch/2386059|title=Moroccan king names new government headed by Aziz Akhannouch|publisher=Anadolu Agency|date=7 October 2021|accessdate=14 October 2021}}</ref>

== Cabinet of Aziz Akhannouch (2021–present) ==
The '''Cabinet of Aziz Akhannouch''' is the eleventh government of the Kingdom of Morocco. It was formed on 7 October 2021, following the [[2021 Moroccan general election]], and is headed by [[Aziz Akhannouch]] under the reign of [[Mohammed VI of Morocco|King Mohammed VI]].

The government is composed of ministers from a coalition of three political parties: the [[National Rally of Independents]] (RNI), the [[Authenticity and Modernity Party]] (PAM), and the [[Istiqlal Party]] (PI). It succeeded the Cabinet of [[Saadeddine Othmani|Saad-Eddine El Othmani]].

The formation of the government was accompanied by a reorganization of several ministries, including the renaming, merging, and redistribution of certain portfolios.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Voici la liste officielle des ministres du gouvernement Akhannouch |url=https://telquel.ma/2021/10/07/voici-la-liste-officielle-des-ministres-du-gouvernement-akhannouch_1739427?fbrefresh=5 |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=Telquel.ma |language=fr}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |title=King Mohammed VI Receives New Ministers as Part of Government Reshuffle |url=https://www.moroccoworldnews.com/2024/10/365910/king-mohammed-vi-

The '''Cabinet of Morocco''' is the chief executive body of the [[Morocco|Kingdom of Morocco]]. The Cabinet is composed of some 24 ministers including 5 "Minister Delegates". It is headed by the [[Prime Minister of Morocco|Prime Minister]] since August 2011, who is appointed by the [[King of Morocco]] from the [[List of political parties in Morocco|party]] that achieved a [[Plurality (voting)|plurality]] in the [[Elections in Morocco|parliamentary elections]]. The Cabinet's ministers are chosen by the PM, after consultation with other parties forming the Government coalition, then validated and appointed by the King. Ldm1954::Friction.diff_added:
[[File:Asperities.svg|thumb|The top image shows asperities under no load. The bottom image depicts the same surface after applying a load.]]
Friction is due to a range of energy dissipative processes. The base model, first detailed by Bowden and Tabor<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Bowden |first=F P |url=https://academic.oup.com/book/54811 |title=The Friction and Lubrication of Solids |last2=Tabor |first2=D |date=2001-02-08 |publisher=Oxford University PressOxford |isbn=978-0-19-850777-2 |language=en |doi=10.1093/oso/9780198507772.001.0001}}</ref> is that it involves contact of small protusions on surfaces called [[asperities]]. As the load increases the number and the area of asperity contacts increases, leading to the normal force dependence of friction. As these asperities slide past each other both [[Plastic Deformation|plastic]] and [[elastic deformation]] processes take place, among others. The frictional force is the energy dissipated per unit distance by these.<ref name=":0" />

Experimental studies of dry sliding friction in engineering materials have shown that friction and wear are controlled not only by the nominal contact conditions, but also by near-surface microstructural evolution, debris generation and tribochemical transformations during sliding. Reviews and experimental studies have shown that plastic deformation, fracture, material transfer, mechanical mixing and oxidation can all contribute to the transition between mild and severe wear regimes in dry metal contacts.<ref>{{cite journal
}}</ref><ref>{{cite book
|last=Hutchings
|first=I. M.
|title=Tribology: Friction and Wear of Engineering Materials
|publisher=Butterworth-Heinemann
|year=1992
|isbn=9780750610544
}}</ref><ref>{{cite book
|last=Zum Gahr
|first=K.-H.
|title=Microstructure and Wear of Materials
|publisher=Elsevier
|year=1987
|isbn=9780444427540
}}</ref>

The importance of debris and “third-body” material formed in the contact has also been demonstrated experimentally, since compacted triboparticulates may either accelerate damage or form protective interfacial layers that reduce direct metal-to-metal contact.<ref>{{cite journal
}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal
}}</ref> Experimental investigations further indicate that environmental factors such as hydrogen charging, lubrication regime and cyclic loading may significantly influence crack initiation mechanisms and friction stability in structural alloys.<ref>{{cite journal
}}</ref>
In 2008 scientists for the first time were able to move a single atom across a surface, and measure the forces required. Using ultrahigh vacuum and nearly zero temperature (5 K), a modified atomic force microscope was used to drag a [[cobalt]] atom, and a [[carbon monoxide]] molecule, across surfaces of copper and [[platinum]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ternes |first1=Markus |last2=Lutz |first2=Christopher P. |last3=Hirjibehedin |first3=Cyrus F. |last4=Giessibl |first4=Franz J. |author-link5= Andreas J. Heinrich |last5=Heinrich |first5=Andrea

'''Dirk Jan Klaas Huntelaar''' (born 12 August 1983), known professionally as '''Klaas-Jan Huntelaar''' ({{IPA|nl|ˌklaːˈɕjɑn  ˈɦʏntəlaːr|-|Klaas-Jan Huntelaar pronunciation.ogg}}),{{efn|In isolation, ''Klaas'' and ''Jan'' are pronounced {{IPA|nl|klaːs|}} and {{IPA|nl|jɑn|}}, respectively.}} is a Dutch former professional [[association football|footballer]] who played as a [[striker (association football)|striker]].
Huntelaar played for [[PSV Eindhoven|PSV]], [[De Graafschap]], [[AGOVV Apeldoorn]], [[SC Heerenveen|Heerenveen]], [[AFC Ajax|Ajax]], [[Real Madrid CF|Real Madrid]] and [[A.C. EmyGF::Ikariam.comment: Removed Maintenance Template from "Alliances" and re-wrote the entire section with additional and more specific details from Help section of the game (cannot be linked).  I am editing this content as an employee of Gameforge
EmyGF::Ikariam.diff_added:
''Ikariam'' is nominally [[free-to-play]] but players may opt to purchase in-game advantages via a [[Microtransaction#In MMORPGs|Microtransaction]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.worldsinmotion.biz/2008/08/online_world_atlas_ikariam_pt_2.php|title=Online World Atlas: Ikariam -- Pt. 3, Conclusion|author=K., Matthew|publisher=[[Gamasutra|Worlds in Motion]]|date=2008-08-19|access-date=2009-02-11|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221103335/http://www.worldsinmotion.biz/2008/08/online_world_atlas_ikariam_pt_2.php|archive-date=2009-02-21|url-status=dead}}</ref> system based on Ambrosia, the game's premium currency, which allows them to speed up processes and gain various advantages, including access to Ikariam Plus.
Once the Academy Research “Foreign Cultures”, which can be found in the “Seafaring” area of the Academy, is completed, an Embassy can be built. From the Embassy building, players can found their own alliance or apply to join an existing one. Alliances are groups of players who cooperate and support each other.<ref name="Ikariam vs. Travian" /> They will usually protect one another; if a member is attacked, stronger members or the entire alliance may intervene to defend against the attacker.

The maximum number of members an alliance can have depends on the level of the leader’s Embassy. Additionally, alliances generate a small daily bonus of resources based on their total number of members.

Each alliance includes a leadership structure consisting of one leader and several special roles. The leader has full administrative control and can distribute special posts, transfer leadership, edit alliance details (such as name, tag, language, and description), and disband the alliance. The leader can also manage diplomatic points by transferring free personal diplomatic points to the alliance or returning unused alliance diplomatic points to their account. Like all members, the leader benefits from the alliance’s resource bonus.

There are three assignable special roles within an alliance: Diplomat, General, and Home Secretary.

The Diplomat is responsible for managing external relations. This role can edit the public alliance page and receives messages sent to the alliance from external players or other alliances.

The General oversees military coordination. This includes viewing the total troop strength of each member, monitoring incoming attacks against alliance members, and managing alliance military actions, including the ability to withdraw attacks initiated by members.

The Home Secretary manages internal organization. This role can view the total resources of each member, create and manage custom alliance ranks, assign those ranks to members, and edit the internal alliance page. The Home Secretary also receives and manages applications from players wishing to join the alliance.

'''Cymin Samawatie''' (* [[Braunschweig]], Germany) is a German musician of [[Iranian peoples|Iranian]] background. She is known as singer, composer, conductor and founder of several ensembles for [[Ethno jazz|ethno-jazz]] and experimental music in Germany. She has written compositions for a variety of ensembles – from [[chamber music]] to [[Big band|big bands]] – including commissions for the [[Morgenland Festival Osnabrück|Morgenland Festival]] [[Morgenland Festival Osnabrück|Osnabrück]] and the [[Berlin Philharmonic]]. Her work has been honoured with awards including the German World Music Award, the German Jazz Award and the Prize for Cultural Promotion.
In 2002, she founded the jazz quartet [[Cyminology]], combining contemporary jazz with [[Persian poetry]]. In 2003, she performed alongside the vocalist [[Bobby McFerrin]] at a concert in her home town Braunschweig.<ref name=":2">{{cite web|access-date=2026-03-25 |date=2008-07-01 |language=en |title=14º Festival Internacional de Jazz de Loulé |url=https://www.ccloule.com/festival-de-jazz-de-loule-2008-en}}<!-- auto-translated from German by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> In 2013, Samawatie along with musicians Ketan Bhatti and Philip Geisler founded the Diwan der Kontinente orchestra, later renamed [[Trickster Orchestra]]. This ensemble comprises instrumentalists from the [[Berlin Philharmonic]] and musicians from [[Syria]], [[Japan]], [[China]], [[Israel]], [[Lebanon]], [[Siberia]] and [[Turkey]]. The ensemble aims to translate the concept of the big band into contemporary and hybrid, post-migrant forms characterised by [[cultural diversity]] and the breaking down of identities.<ref name=":0">{{cite web|access-date=2026-03-25 |date=2025-06-25 |format=PDF |language=de |title=Cymin Samawatie & Ketan Bhatti erhalten den Friedlieb Ferdinand Runge-Preis für unkonventionelle Kunstvermittlung 2025 |url=https://berlinischeg
